title: Ready for the world
key_points: Tan's central thesis: a directional 'floating charge of energy' (Jung/von Franz; 'stagnant chi' in Chinese medicine) is a signal to make something 'unmistakably yours', by following curiosity, using what you already have, and changing as the work asks of you.

Her book 'Ask This Book a Question' is a choose-your-own-adventure on decision-making, designed entirely in Figma; readers route a stuck question through lenses of topic/subtopic, timing (urgent vs. once-in-a-lifetime), then land on a personal story where biases are named in situ with their 'light and shadow.'

Key reframe from her friend's job-quitting struggle: explaining cognitive biases ('loss aversion,' 'status quo bias') doesn't change behavior, people need to feel and place themselves in it. The 'denomination effect' (overweighting big things, undervaluing small recurring ones) was the breakthrough Mapo Tofu story.

Repeated publisher feedback that her polished, professional illustrations and covers 'didn't match her voice' led her to use her own unrefined illustrations and non-literary writing, the imperfection was what made it authentically hers.

She used the four-part Japanese/Chinese poetic structure Kishotenketsu (no climactic resolution like the hero's journey), change comes from an internal 'turn' (noticing) rather than external conflict; illustrated by the hardest, most personal story (Pentel 0.5mm lead / her late father).

quotes.text: It wasn't perfect, but it was unmistakably mine.
When the floating charge of energy finds its direction, the question stops following you, and you start following it.
